title: Imports, exports and module graph

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Imports, exports and module graph; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Named and default exports form explicit module interfaces, and imports are statically analyzable live bindings.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A utility module exports parse and format by name so callers import only the declared interface. Your goal is to organize a feature into modules and diagnose an import or cycle problem. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Control flow and functions; Module loading baseline; Lexical scope and temporal dead zone. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Named and default exports form explicit module interfaces, and imports are statically analyzable live bindings. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Default naming is local, re-exports can cause cycles, and dynamic import returns a promise. This draft therefore rejects: A module may have several default exports with different names.

includes: Create explicit named or default exports and understand live bindings and cycles conceptually.
Organize a feature into modules and diagnose an import or cycle problem.
excludes: Default naming is local, re-exports can cause cycles, and dynamic import returns a promise.

outcomes: Organize a feature into modules and diagnose an import or cycle problem.

examples.title: Imports, exports and module graph: concrete case
examples.situation: A utility module exports parse and format by name so callers import only the declared interface.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A utility module exports parse and format by name so callers import only the declared interface.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: A module has at most one default export; use named exports for multiple explicit interface members.
examples.result: The result follows because A module has at most one default export; use named exports for multiple explicit interface members.

examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: A module has at most one default export; use named exports for multiple explicit interface members.
